model.openbuild_dbt.mart_product_concent.name: mart_product_concentration
model.openbuild_dbt.mart_product_concent.description:
Pareto / concentration analysis. 8 rows (one per product) ordered by revenue descending. Includes per-product revenue share AND cumulative revenue share across the catalog. Powers the Top-3 concentration finding (top 3 products = 85% of revenue).
Note: 33 NULL-usd_price orders excluded at the CTE WHERE clause, consistent with README data-quality decisions.

product_name.name: product_name
product_name.description: Primary key. Foreign key to dim_product.

orders.name: orders
orders.description: Number of non-NULL-price orders for this product.

net_revenue.name: net_revenue
net_revenue.description: Sum of usd_price for non-refunded orders.

avg_aov.name: avg_aov
avg_aov.description: Average order value for non-refunded orders.

pct_of_orders.name: pct_of_orders
pct_of_orders.description: This product's share of all orders. Bounded [0, 100].

pct_of_revenue.name: pct_of_revenue
pct_of_revenue.description: This product's share of all net revenue. Bounded [0, 100].

cumulative_pct_of_revenue.name: cumulative_pct_of_revenue
cumulative_pct_of_revenue.description: Cumulative net-revenue share, in revenue-descending order. Monotonically increasing. The last row equals 100 (within floating-point precision).

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_product_concent.raw_code:
-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       mart_product_concentration
-- Layer:       Gold (marts)
-- Source:      fct_orders (gold)
-- Grain:       One row per product (8 rows total)
-- Description: Pareto / concentration analysis. Computes per-product
--              revenue share AND cumulative revenue share across the
--              ranked product list. Cumulative column powers the
--              "top N products = X% of revenue" finding.
--              Powers README Finding 4: top 3 products ~85% of revenue.
--
-- Note: 33 NULL-usd_price orders excluded at CTE level (consistent
-- with README data-quality decision: excluded from revenue aggregations).
-- =====================================================================

WITH product_revenue AS (

    SELECT
        product_name,
        COUNT(*) AS orders,
        SUM(usd_price) FILTER (WHERE is_refunded = 0) AS net_revenue,
        SUM(usd_price)                                 AS gross_revenue,
        AVG(usd_price) FILTER (WHERE is_refunded = 0) AS avg_aov
    FROM {{ ref('fct_orders') }}
    WHERE usd_price IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY product_name

)

SELECT
    product_name,
    orders,
    net_revenue,
    avg_aov,
    100.0 * orders      / SUM(orders)      OVER ()                       AS pct_of_orders,
    100.0 * net_revenue / SUM(net_revenue) OVER ()                       AS pct_of_revenue,
    100.0 * SUM(net_revenue) OVER (
        ORDER BY net_revenue DESC
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) / SUM(net_revenue) OVER ()                                         AS cumulative_pct_of_revenue
FROM product_revenue
ORDER BY net_revenue DESC

model.openbuild_dbt.dim_country.name: dim_country
model.openbuild_dbt.dim_country.description: One row per country code. 193 rows = 191 from stg_country_lookup + 2 synthetic rows ('EU', 'AP') absorbing 18 orders that use region codes as country codes (README data quality decision).

country_code.name: country_code
country_code.description: Primary key. ISO country code or synthetic region code.

region.name: region
region.description: Regional rollup. AMER, EMEA, APAC, LATAM, or Unclassified.

model.openbuild_dbt.dim_country.raw_code:
-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       dim_country
-- Layer:       Gold (marts)
-- Source:      stg_country_lookup (silver)
-- Grain:       One row per country_code
-- Description: Country dimension with regional rollup. Includes 2 synthetic
--              rows ("EU", "AP") to absorb 18 orders that use region codes
--              as country codes (documented in README data quality).
--              Coverage: 191 source countries + 2 synthetic = 193 rows.
-- =====================================================================

WITH source_with_synthetic AS (

    SELECT country_code, region
    FROM {{ ref('stg_country_lookup') }}
    WHERE country_code IS NOT NULL

    UNION ALL

    SELECT 'EU' AS country_code, 'Unclassified' AS region

    UNION ALL

    SELECT 'AP' AS country_code, 'Unclassified' AS region

)

SELECT
    country_code,
    region
FROM source_with_synthetic

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_refund_metrics.name: mart_refund_metrics
model.openbuild_dbt.mart_refund_metrics.description: Refund analysis by (product, country). 841 rows. Provides dual perspective: count-based refund_rate_pct and value-based refunded_revenue_pct. The two can diverge meaningfully when higher-AOV orders refund at different rates than the average. Rows with NULL country_code (154 orders across 4 products) cluster into 4 rows of this output.

country_code.name: country_code
country_code.description: Foreign key to dim_country. ~4 rows have NULL country_code aggregating the 154 source orders without a country.

orders.name: orders
orders.description: Number of orders in this (product, country).

refunds.name: refunds
refunds.description: Number of orders in this group that were refunded.

gross_revenue_usd.name: gross_revenue_usd
gross_revenue_usd.description: Sum of usd_price for orders in this group (includes refunds).

refunded_revenue_usd.name: refunded_revenue_usd
refunded_revenue_usd.description: Sum of usd_price for refunded orders only.

net_revenue_usd.name: net_revenue_usd
net_revenue_usd.description: Sum of usd_price for non-refunded orders only.

refunded_revenue_pct.name: refunded_revenue_pct
refunded_revenue_pct.description: Percent of revenue refunded. Can be NULL if all orders had NULL usd_price (defensive NULLIF). Otherwise bounded [0, 100].

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_refund_metrics.raw_code:
-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       mart_refund_metrics
-- Layer:       Gold (marts)
-- Source:      fct_orders (gold)
-- Grain:       One row per (product_name, country_code)
-- Description: Refund analysis with both count-based (refund_rate_pct)
--              and value-based (refunded_revenue_pct) angles. Dual
--              perspective surfaces cases where refund rate doesn't
--              tell the same story as refund value.
--
-- Note: ~154 orders have NULL country_code; they appear as rows
-- where country_code IS NULL in this output (one per product).
-- =====================================================================

SELECT
    product_name,
    country_code,
    COUNT(*)                                                         AS orders,
    SUM(is_refunded)                                                 AS refunds,
    ROUND(100.0 * SUM(is_refunded) / COUNT(*), 2)                    AS refund_rate_pct,
    ROUND(SUM(usd_price), 2)                                         AS gross_revenue_usd,
    ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN is_refunded = 1 THEN usd_price ELSE 0 END), 2) AS refunded_revenue_usd,
    ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN is_refunded = 0 THEN usd_price ELSE 0 END), 2) AS net_revenue_usd,
    ROUND(
        100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN is_refunded = 1 THEN usd_price ELSE 0 END)
              / NULLIF(SUM(usd_price), 0),
        2
    )                                                                AS refunded_revenue_pct

FROM {{ ref('fct_orders') }}
GROUP BY product_name, country_code

model.openbuild_dbt.dim_users.name: dim_users
model.openbuild_dbt.dim_users.description: One row per user. 87,625 users. THE METHODOLOGICALLY CRITICAL MODEL OF THE PROJECT: loyalty_status, marketing_channel, device, and first_purchase_aov are SNAPSHOTTED AT FIRST PURCHASE to avoid reverse causality. README documents this as the single most important methodological choice. Tie-breaker on order_id ensures deterministic results across runs.

first_purchase_ts.name: first_purchase_ts
first_purchase_ts.description: Timestamp of the user's first order.

first_purchase_month.name: first_purchase_month
first_purchase_month.description: First-day-of-month truncation of first_purchase_ts. Used as cohort_month downstream.

loyalty_at_first_purchase.name: loyalty_at_first_purchase
loyalty_at_first_purchase.description: Loyalty status (0/1) on the user's first order. NEVER updated after first purchase; this is the snapshot that powers the loyalty retention finding.

marketing_channel_at_first_purchase.name: marketing_channel_at_first_purchase
marketing_channel_at_first_purchase.description: Marketing channel attributed to the user at first purchase. ~1,153 NULL after NaN coercion at silver.

device_at_first_purchase.name: device_at_first_purchase
device_at_first_purchase.description: Account creation device captured at the user's first purchase. ~1,153 NULL after NaN coercion at silver.

first_purchase_aov.name: first_purchase_aov
first_purchase_aov.description: USD price of the user's first-purchase order. ~27 NULL where the user's first order had no recorded price.

model.openbuild_dbt.dim_users.raw_code:
-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       dim_users
-- Layer:       Gold (marts)
-- Source:      stg_orders (silver)
-- Grain:       One row per user
-- Description: User dimension with attributes SNAPSHOTTED AT FIRST
--              PURCHASE. This is the single most important methodological
--              choice in the project: by fixing loyalty, marketing channel,
--              and device to their values at the user's first order, we
--              avoid the reverse-causality trap where current-state
--              attributes are contaminated by retention outcomes.
--              Reference: README "Data Quality Decisions" section.
--
--              Tie-breaking: when a user has multiple orders sharing the
--              same earliest purchase_ts (common because bronze timestamps
--              are at midnight), we deterministically pick the row with
--              the lexically smallest order_id.
-- =====================================================================

WITH first_orders AS (

    -- Each user's earliest purchase timestamp
    SELECT
        user_id,
        MIN(purchase_ts) AS first_purchase_ts
    FROM {{ ref('stg_orders') }}
    GROUP BY user_id

),

first_attrs AS (

    -- For each user, capture loyalty/channel/device/price from their
    -- earliest order. Tie-broken on order_id for determinism.
    SELECT DISTINCT
        user_id,
        FIRST_VALUE(loyalty_status)
            OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY purchase_ts, order_id) AS loyalty_at_first_purchase,
        FIRST_VALUE(marketing_channel)
            OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY purchase_ts, order_id) AS marketing_channel_at_first_purchase,
        FIRST_VALUE(account_creation_device)
            OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY purchase_ts, order_id) AS device_at_first_purchase,
        FIRST_VALUE(usd_price)
            OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY purchase_ts, order_id) AS first_purchase_aov
    FROM {{ ref('stg_orders') }}

)

SELECT
    f.user_id,
    f.first_purchase_ts,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', f.first_purchase_ts)::DATE  AS first_purchase_month,
    a.loyalty_at_first_purchase,
    a.marketing_channel_at_first_purchase,
    a.device_at_first_purchase,
    a.first_purchase_aov

FROM first_orders f
INNER JOIN first_attrs a USING (user_id)

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_channel_revenue.name: mart_channel_revenue
model.openbuild_dbt.mart_channel_revenue.description: Monthly revenue by platform. 96 rows (48 months x 2 platforms). Includes orders count, gross and net revenue, and within-month share of net revenue. Supports the dashboard's channel-mix and platform-trend analyses.

purchase_month.name: purchase_month
purchase_month.description: First day of the month the orders were placed.

purchase_platform.name: purchase_platform
purchase_platform.description: Platform the orders came from. Foreign key to dim_platform.

orders.name: orders
orders.description: Number of orders in this (month, platform).

gross_revenue_usd.name: gross_revenue_usd
gross_revenue_usd.description: Sum of usd_price for all orders (including refunded).

net_revenue_usd.name: net_revenue_usd
net_revenue_usd.description: Sum of usd_price for non-refunded orders only.

share_of_month_pct.name: share_of_month_pct
share_of_month_pct.description: This platform's share of the month's total net revenue. Bounded [0, 100].

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_channel_revenue.raw_code:
-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       mart_channel_revenue
-- Layer:       Gold (marts)
-- Source:      fct_orders (gold)
-- Grain:       One row per (purchase_month, purchase_platform)
-- Description: Monthly revenue by platform with within-month share.
--              Supports channel-mix and platform-trend analysis on
--              the dashboard.
-- =====================================================================

WITH monthly AS (

    SELECT
        purchase_month,
        purchase_platform,
        COUNT(*)                                                          AS orders,
        ROUND(SUM(usd_price), 2)                                          AS gross_revenue_usd,
        ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN is_refunded = 0 THEN usd_price ELSE 0 END), 2) AS net_revenue_usd
    FROM {{ ref('fct_orders') }}
    GROUP BY purchase_month, purchase_platform

),

monthly_total AS (

    SELECT
        purchase_month,
        SUM(net_revenue_usd) AS total_month_net_revenue
    FROM monthly
    GROUP BY purchase_month

)

SELECT
    m.purchase_month,
    m.purchase_platform,
    m.orders,
    m.gross_revenue_usd,
    m.net_revenue_usd,
    ROUND(100.0 * m.net_revenue_usd / NULLIF(t.total_month_net_revenue, 0), 2) AS share_of_month_pct

FROM monthly m
INNER JOIN monthly_total t USING (purchase_month)
ORDER BY m.purchase_month, m.purchase_platform

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_marketing_acqui.name: mart_marketing_acquisition
model.openbuild_dbt.mart_marketing_acqui.description:
Acquisition mix at first purchase by (channel, loyalty_status). 11 rows (5 channels + 1 NULL bucket, x 2 loyalty values; some combos may not exist).
Powers README finding 1b: email channel disproportionately acquires loyalty members AND low-AOV buyers. Note the interaction: within email, loyalty -> higher AOV; within direct, loyalty -> lower AOV. Channel and loyalty are not independent.
Note: 27 users with NULL first_purchase_aov excluded (matches README revenue-aggregation rules). 1,153 users with NULL marketing_channel cluster into a NULL row.

marketing_channel.name: marketing_channel
marketing_channel.description: Snapshotted marketing_channel_at_first_purchase from dim_users. May be NULL for users where the source had 'NaN'.

users_acquired.name: users_acquired
users_acquired.description: Count of users in this (channel, loyalty) cell.

avg_first_aov.name: avg_first_aov
avg_first_aov.description: Average first_purchase_aov for users in this cell.

total_first_revenue.name: total_first_revenue
total_first_revenue.description: Sum of first_purchase_aov for users in this cell.

pct_within_channel.name: pct_within_channel
pct_within_channel.description: This cell's share of its channel's total users. Bounded [0, 100]. By construction, the pct_within_channel values for the two loyalty rows of any single channel sum to 100.

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_marketing_acqui.raw_code:
-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       mart_marketing_acquisition
-- Layer:       Gold (marts)
-- Source:      dim_users (gold)
-- Grain:       One row per (marketing_channel, loyalty_status)
-- Description: Shows which marketing channels disproportionately recruit
--              loyalty members and the AOV signal at acquisition by
--              channel/loyalty combination. Powers README finding 1b:
--              email channel concentrates loyalty signups AND low-AOV
--              buyers (suggesting a channel-mix confounder behind the
--              headline loyalty retention finding).
--
-- Note: dim_users provides snapshot-at-first-purchase values. Filtering
-- first_purchase_aov IS NOT NULL drops 27 users whose first order had
-- NULL price, consistent with README data-quality decisions.
-- The ~1,153 users with NULL marketing_channel cluster into a NULL row.
-- =====================================================================

SELECT
    marketing_channel_at_first_purchase                              AS marketing_channel,
    loyalty_at_first_purchase                                        AS loyalty_status,
    COUNT(*)                                                         AS users_acquired,
    AVG(first_purchase_aov)                                          AS avg_first_aov,
    SUM(first_purchase_aov)                                          AS total_first_revenue,
    100.0 * COUNT(*) / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (
        PARTITION BY marketing_channel_at_first_purchase
    )                                                                AS pct_within_channel

FROM {{ ref('dim_users') }}
WHERE first_purchase_aov IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY marketing_channel_at_first_purchase, loyalty_at_first_purchase
ORDER BY marketing_channel, loyalty_status

    loyalty_at_first_purchase                                        AS loyalty_status,

FROM "dev"."gold"."dim_users"
WHERE first_purchase_aov IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY marketing_channel_at_first_purchase, loyalty_at_first_purchase

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_cohort_retentio.name: mart_cohort_retention
model.openbuild_dbt.mart_cohort_retentio.description:
Long-format cohort retention matrix. 1,049 rows. Grain: (cohort_month, months_since_acquisition).
Powers the README cohort heatmap (data/outputs/cohort_retention_heatmap.png): 48 cohorts on the y-axis, months_since_acquisition on the x-axis, retention_pct as color intensity.
The data is right-censored: late cohorts (e.g. 2022-12) have fewer observable months than the data window allows. Cells exist only where at least one user was active in that period.
Note: this mart agrees with mart_loyalty_retention at the cohort_month level. Sum of cohort sizes across loyalty values in mart_loyalty_retention equals cohort_size here.

months_since_acquisition.name: months_since_acquisition
months_since_acquisition.description: Months between cohort_month and the order's purchase_month. 0 = acquisition month; non-negative.

active_users.name: active_users
active_users.description: Distinct users in this cohort with at least one order in this period.

cohort_size.name: cohort_size
cohort_size.description: Total users acquired in this cohort_month. Derived from active_users WHERE months_since_acquisition = 0 (every acquired user has a first order in their acquisition month).

retention_pct.name: retention_pct
retention_pct.description: 100 * active_users / cohort_size. Bounded [0, 100]. At months_since_acquisition = 0, always 100.

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_cohort_retentio.raw_code:
-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       mart_cohort_retention
-- Layer:       Gold (marts)
-- Source:      fct_orders (gold)
-- Grain:       One row per (cohort_month, months_since_acquisition)
-- Description: Cohort retention matrix in long format. Powers the
--              heatmap visualization in the README (cohort x month grid).
--
--              The cohort denominator is derived from
--              active_users WHERE months_since_acquisition = 0, which
--              equals the number of users acquired in that cohort_month
--              (every acquired user has a first order by definition).
--
-- Right-censoring: cells exist only where at least one user was active.
-- Late cohorts (e.g. 2022-11, 2022-12) have fewer observable months
-- than the data window allows. Downstream consumers (heatmap charts)
-- must mask these gaps as needed.
-- =====================================================================

WITH active_users AS (

    SELECT
        cohort_month,
        months_since_acquisition,
        COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)                       AS active_users
    FROM {{ ref('fct_orders') }}
    GROUP BY cohort_month, months_since_acquisition

),

cohort_size AS (

    SELECT
        cohort_month,
        active_users                                  AS cohort_size
    FROM active_users
    WHERE months_since_acquisition = 0

)

SELECT
    a.cohort_month,
    a.months_since_acquisition,
    a.active_users,
    s.cohort_size,
    ROUND(a.active_users * 100.0 / s.cohort_size, 2) AS retention_pct
FROM active_users a
INNER JOIN cohort_size s USING (cohort_month)
ORDER BY a.cohort_month, a.months_since_acquisition

model.openbuild_dbt.first_purchase_summa.name: first_purchase_summary
model.openbuild_dbt.first_purchase_summa.description:
Count of users whose FIRST purchase was a given product, split by loyalty status at acquisition. Up to 16 rows max (2 loyalty values x 8 products); actual is 15 (no loyalty members chose Apple iPhone as a first purchase).
Powers README finding 1a mechanism: "loyalty disproportionately recruits AirPods buyers (58.3% of loyalty first-purchases) and underweights replenishables (5.7% Charging Cable Pack)." This mart is the evidence behind the product-mix explanation for the loyalty retention deficit.

product_name.name: product_name
product_name.description: Product name of the user's first purchase. FK to dim_product.

first_orders.name: first_orders
first_orders.description: Number of users whose first purchase was this product.

model.openbuild_dbt.first_purchase_summa.raw_code:
-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       first_purchase_summary
-- Layer:       Gold (marts)
-- Sources:     fct_orders (gold), dim_users (gold)
-- Grain:       One row per (loyalty_status, product_name)
-- Description: Counts users by the product of their FIRST purchase,
--              split by loyalty status at acquisition. Up to 16 rows
--              (2 loyalty values x 8 products); fewer if some combos
--              never occurred (e.g. no loyalty + bose soundsport users).
--
-- Powers README finding 1a mechanism: "loyalty disproportionately
-- recruits AirPods buyers (58.1% of loyalty first-purchases) and
-- underweights replenishables (5.7% Charging Cable Pack)."
--
-- Why this mart matters: the loyalty 3.6x retention deficit (per
-- mart_loyalty_retention) is caused by product-mix differences at
-- acquisition, not by the loyalty program itself. This mart is the
-- evidence that justifies that mechanism claim.
-- =====================================================================

SELECT
    u.loyalty_at_first_purchase                       AS loyalty_status,
    f.product_name,
    COUNT(*)                                          AS first_orders

FROM {{ ref('fct_orders') }} f
INNER JOIN {{ ref('dim_users') }} u USING (user_id)

WHERE f.is_first_purchase = 1
GROUP BY u.loyalty_at_first_purchase, f.product_name
ORDER BY u.loyalty_at_first_purchase, first_orders DESC

model.openbuild_dbt.fct_orders.name: fct_orders
model.openbuild_dbt.fct_orders.description: Order-grain fact table. 108,124 rows. Join point of the gold layer: stg_orders joined to dim_users for cohort attribution, with derived columns powering downstream marts. Methodological note: cohort_month derives from the USER's first purchase month (via dim_users), NOT this order's own month. This preserves the snapshot logic that prevents reverse-causality contamination in retention analyses.

cohort_month.name: cohort_month
cohort_month.description: User's first-purchase month, copied from dim_users. NOT this order's purchase month. Cohort assignment is fixed at user acquisition and never moves.

months_since_acquisition.name: months_since_acquisition
months_since_acquisition.description: Months between cohort_month and purchase_month. Zero for the user's first purchase; increases for subsequent orders. Always non-negative (an order can't precede its own cohort).

user_order_seq.name: user_order_seq
user_order_seq.description: 1-based rank of this order within the user's purchase history. Tie-broken by order_id.

is_first_purchase.name: is_first_purchase
is_first_purchase.description: 1 if user_order_seq=1, else 0. Convenient flag for acquisition-order analyses.

is_refunded.name: is_refunded
is_refunded.description: Inherited from stg_orders. 1 if refund_ts populated, else 0.

loyalty_status.name: loyalty_status
loyalty_status.description: Order-time loyalty status (NOT snapshotted - this is the value on this specific order). Compare to dim_users.loyalty_at_first_purchase for cohort-vs-current analyses.

country_code.name: country_code
country_code.description: Foreign key to dim_country. dim_country includes 2 synthetic rows (EU, AP) absorbing region-codes-as-country-codes orders. ~154 orders have NULL country_code (source data quality issue not previously documented; surfaced by dbt migration).

usd_price.name: usd_price
usd_price.description: Inherited from stg_orders. ~33 NULL prices upstream.

model.openbuild_dbt.fct_orders.raw_code:
-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       fct_orders
-- Layer:       Gold (marts)
-- Sources:     stg_orders (silver), dim_users (gold)
-- Grain:       One row per order
-- Description: Order-grain fact table. Joins to dim_users to attach
--              cohort attribution (cohort_month from user first purchase
--              month, NOT this order's month — anti-confounder logic).
--              Includes derived columns for downstream marts:
--                - purchase_month: month of THIS order
--                - cohort_month: user's first-purchase month (cohort key)
--                - months_since_acquisition: order_month - cohort_month
--                - user_order_seq: 1-based rank of order within user
--                - is_first_purchase: 1 if user_order_seq = 1
-- =====================================================================

WITH ranked AS (

    SELECT
        o.*,
        DATE_TRUNC('month', o.purchase_ts)::DATE        AS purchase_month,
        u.first_purchase_ts,
        u.first_purchase_month                          AS cohort_month,
        DATE_DIFF('month',
                  u.first_purchase_month,
                  DATE_TRUNC('month', o.purchase_ts)::DATE
        )                                               AS months_since_acquisition,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY o.user_id
            ORDER BY o.purchase_ts ASC, o.order_id ASC
        )                                               AS user_order_seq
    FROM {{ ref('stg_orders') }} o
    INNER JOIN {{ ref('dim_users') }} u USING (user_id)

)

SELECT
    order_id,
    user_id,
    purchase_ts,
    purchase_month,
    refund_ts,
    is_refunded,
    product_name,
    usd_price,
    purchase_platform,
    country_code,
    loyalty_status,
    first_purchase_ts,
    cohort_month,
    months_since_acquisition,
    user_order_seq,
    CASE WHEN user_order_seq = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END      AS is_first_purchase

FROM ranked

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_loyalty_retenti.name: mart_loyalty_retention
model.openbuild_dbt.mart_loyalty_retenti.description:
Cohort retention table split by loyalty status at acquisition. ~1,514 rows (varies with data). Grain: (cohort_month, loyalty_status, months_since_acquisition).
Powers README finding 1a: loyalty members show 3.6x WORSE month-1 retention than non-loyalty members (0.45% vs 1.63%, simple average across cohorts). Mechanism documented in mart_marketing_acquisition and via product-mix analysis: loyalty disproportionately recruits AirPods buyers (one-and-done), non-loyalty recruits cable-pack buyers (replenishable).
Methodology critical: uses loyalty_at_first_purchase (snapshot) from dim_users to prevent reverse-causality. Using current loyalty status would invert the finding because users who retained had more opportunities to join loyalty later.
Note: rows exist only for (cohort, loyalty, month_N) combos where at least one user was active.

months_since_acquisition.name: months_since_acquisition
months_since_acquisition.description: Months between cohort_month and the order's purchase_month. 0 = acquisition month; non-negative; usually 0-47 across the observation window.

active_users.name: active_users
active_users.description: Distinct users in this cohort cell who placed at least one order in this period.

cohort_size.name: cohort_size
cohort_size.description: Total users in this (cohort_month, loyalty_status). Denominator for retention_pct.

retention_pct.name: retention_pct
retention_pct.description: 100 * active_users / cohort_size. Bounded [0, 100]. At months_since_acquisition = 0, always 100.

model.openbuild_dbt.mart_loyalty_retenti.raw_code:
-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       mart_loyalty_retention
-- Layer:       Gold (marts)
-- Sources:     dim_users (gold), fct_orders (gold)
-- Grain:       One row per (cohort_month, loyalty_status, months_since_acquisition)
-- Description: Cohort retention table split by loyalty status at acquisition.
--              Powers the headline finding: loyalty members retain
--              approximately 7.4x better than non-loyalty members.
--
-- Methodology:
--   - cohort_size from dim_users: denominator fixed at acquisition
--   - active_per_period from fct_orders: counts distinct users with at
--     least one order in (cohort_month, loyalty_status, months_since_acquisition)
--   - retention_pct = 100 * active_users / cohort_size
--
-- Anti-confounder: uses loyalty_at_first_purchase, NOT current loyalty
-- status. Otherwise users who retained would skew loyalty enrollment,
-- producing reverse-causal correlation.
--
-- Note: rows exist only for (cohort, loyalty, month_N) combos where at
-- least one user was active. Months with zero retention have no row;
-- downstream consumers must handle gaps if they need them.
-- =====================================================================

WITH cohort_sizes AS (

    SELECT
        first_purchase_month                          AS cohort_month,
        loyalty_at_first_purchase                     AS loyalty_status,
        COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)                       AS cohort_size
    FROM {{ ref('dim_users') }}
    GROUP BY first_purchase_month, loyalty_at_first_purchase

),

active_per_period AS (

    SELECT
        u.first_purchase_month                        AS cohort_month,
        u.loyalty_at_first_purchase                   AS loyalty_status,
        f.months_since_acquisition,
        COUNT(DISTINCT f.user_id)                     AS active_users
    FROM {{ ref('fct_orders') }} f
    INNER JOIN {{ ref('dim_users') }} u ON f.user_id = u.user_id
    GROUP BY u.first_purchase_month, u.loyalty_at_first_purchase, f.months_since_acquisition

)

SELECT
    a.cohort_month,
    a.loyalty_status,
    a.months_since_acquisition,
    a.active_users,
    c.cohort_size,
    ROUND(100.0 * a.active_users / c.cohort_size, 2)  AS retention_pct
FROM active_per_period a
INNER JOIN cohort_sizes c
    ON a.cohort_month = c.cohort_month
    AND a.loyalty_status = c.loyalty_status
ORDER BY a.cohort_month, a.loyalty_status, a.months_since_acquisition

model.openbuild_dbt.stg_country_lookup.name: stg_country_lookup
model.openbuild_dbt.stg_country_lookup.description:
One row per country code. 191 rows after deduplication. Source defects resolved here (per sources.yml):
  - NULL country_code row dropped (orphan, was region='EMEA')
  - US deduplicated (source had 2 rows with conflicting regions
    'x' and 'North America'; canonicalized to 'AMER')
  - NULL/junk regions mapped to 'Unclassified'
  - US and CA forced to canonical 'AMER' region

region.name: region
region.description: Regional rollup. AMER, EMEA, APAC, LATAM, or Unclassified.

model.openbuild_dbt.stg_country_lookup.raw_code:
{{
  config(
    materialized='view',
    schema='silver'
  )
}}

-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       stg_country_lookup
-- Layer:       Silver (staging)
-- Source:      bronze.country_lookup_raw
-- Grain:       One row per country_code (after deduplication)
-- Description: Cleans the country-to-region mapping.
--
-- Quality rules applied (all documented in README data quality decisions):
--   1. Drop the 1 row with NULL country_code (region='EMEA' orphan)
--   2. Dedup US (source has 2 rows: regions 'x' and 'North America')
--      via remap + SELECT DISTINCT
--   3. Map NULL or junk regions to 'Unclassified' (26 source rows)
--   4. Force US and CA to canonical 'AMER' region (overrides 'x' and
--      'North America' in source)
-- =====================================================================

WITH cleaned AS (
    SELECT
        COUNTRY_CODE AS country_code,
        CASE
            -- Canonicalize US and CA to AMER regardless of source value
            WHEN COUNTRY_CODE IN ('US', 'CA') THEN 'AMER'
            -- Defensive: catch any 'North America' or 'x' regions on
            -- non-US/CA rows (none expected, but stays robust)
            WHEN REGION IN ('North America', 'x') THEN 'AMER'
            -- NULL regions: 26 source countries lacking a region
            WHEN REGION IS NULL THEN 'Unclassified'
            -- Pass through EMEA, APAC, LATAM
            ELSE REGION
        END AS region
    FROM {{ source('bronze', 'country_lookup_raw') }}
    -- Drop 1 orphan row with NULL country_code (documented in sources.yml)
    WHERE COUNTRY_CODE IS NOT NULL
)

SELECT DISTINCT
    country_code,
    region
FROM cleaned

            -- NULL regions: 26 source countries lacking a region

            -- Pass through EMEA, APAC, LATAM
            ELSE REGION
        END AS region
    FROM "dev"."bronze"."country_lookup_raw"
    -- Drop 1 orphan row with NULL country_code (documented in sources.yml)
    WHERE COUNTRY_CODE IS NOT NULL
)

SELECT DISTINCT
    country_code,
    region
FROM cleaned

model.openbuild_dbt.stg_orders.description: One row per order, cleaned and typed. VARCHAR bronze columns cast to proper types. Rows with unparseable purchase timestamps are dropped (3 rows per README data-quality decisions). NULL-price rows (~33) are retained but excluded by downstream revenue calculations.

order_id.name: order_id
order_id.description: Primary key. Unique identifier for an order.

user_id.name: user_id
user_id.description: Foreign key to dim_users. Stored as VARCHAR (not BIGINT) because 332 source rows have user_id values exceeding INT64 range (floating-point coercion artifact in source export). String storage preserves the identifier without data loss. Investigation of the 332 affected rows deferred; see README 'Future work' once dbt migration is complete.

purchase_ts.name: purchase_ts
purchase_ts.description: Timestamp the order was placed. NEVER NULL in silver.

usd_price.name: usd_price
usd_price.description: Price in USD. ~33 rows are NULL (source data quality issue documented in README). Excluded from revenue aggregations.

purchase_platform.name: purchase_platform
purchase_platform.description: Channel the order was placed on.

loyalty_status.name: loyalty_status
loyalty_status.description: 1 if the user was a loyalty member at order time, 0 otherwise.

is_refunded.name: is_refunded
is_refunded.description: Derived flag. 1 if refund_ts is populated, 0 otherwise.

country_code.name: country_code
country_code.description: ISO country code from source (cleaned variant). ~154 source orders lack a country code; surfaced via warn-severity test and preserved through silver/gold layers as NULL.

marketing_channel.name: marketing_channel
marketing_channel.description: Marketing channel at acquisition. Source 'NaN' strings coerced to SQL NULL in silver (~1,153 rows). Accepted values tested in dim_users.

account_creation_device.name: account_creation_device
account_creation_device.description: Device the user's account was created on. Source had 'NaN' string for ~1,387 NULL rows; coerced to SQL NULL in silver. Used in dim_users for the device-at-acquisition snapshot that supports the loyalty retention finding.

refund_ts.name: refund_ts
refund_ts.description: Timestamp the order was refunded. NULL if not refunded.

ship_ts.name: ship_ts
ship_ts.description: Timestamp the order was shipped. NULL if not yet shipped.

delivery_ts.name: delivery_ts
delivery_ts.description: Timestamp the order was delivered. NULL if not yet delivered.

account_created_on.name: account_created_on
account_created_on.description: Timestamp the user's account was created.

product_name.name: product_name
product_name.description: Human-readable product name. Tested in dim_product.

model.openbuild_dbt.stg_orders.raw_code:
{{
  config(
    materialized='view',
    schema='silver'
  )
}}

-- =====================================================================
-- Model:       stg_orders
-- Layer:       Silver (staging)
-- Source:      bronze.orders_raw
-- Grain:       One row per order
-- Description: Cleaned, typed orders. Casts VARCHAR bronze columns to
--              proper types. Rows with unparseable purchase timestamps
--              are dropped (~3 rows per README data-quality decisions).
--              Uses _cleaned columns where source provides them; raw
--              variants stay in bronze for audit lineage.
-- =====================================================================

SELECT
    -- Identifiers
    USER_ID                                                AS user_id,
    ORDER_ID                                               AS order_id,

    -- Timestamps (cleaned, with safe casting)
    TRY_CAST(PURCHASE_TS_cleaned AS TIMESTAMP)             AS purchase_ts,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', TRY_CAST(PURCHASE_TS_cleaned AS TIMESTAMP))
                                                           AS purchase_month,
    TRY_CAST(REFUND_TS AS TIMESTAMP)                       AS refund_ts,
    TRY_CAST(SHIP_TS AS TIMESTAMP)                         AS ship_ts,
    TRY_CAST(DELIVERY_TS AS TIMESTAMP)                     AS delivery_ts,
    TRY_CAST(CREATED_ON AS TIMESTAMP)                      AS account_created_on,

    -- Order context
    PRODUCT_NAME                                           AS product_name,
    PRODUCT_ID                                              AS product_id,
    TRY_CAST(USD_PRICE_cleaned AS DOUBLE)                  AS usd_price,
    PURCHASE_PLATFORM                                      AS purchase_platform,
    NULLIF(MARKETING_CHANNEL_cleaned, 'NaN')              AS marketing_channel,
    NULLIF(ACCOUNT_CREATION_METHOD_cleaned, 'NaN')         AS account_creation_device,
    COUNTRY_CODE_cleaned                                   AS country_code,
    CAST(LOYALTY_PROGRAM AS INTEGER)                       AS loyalty_status,

    -- Derived flag for downstream refund analysis
    CASE
        WHEN REFUND_TS IS NOT NULL AND TRIM(REFUND_TS) <> '' THEN 1
        ELSE 0
    END                                                    AS is_refunded

FROM {{ source('bronze', 'orders_raw') }}

-- Drop rows where purchase timestamp can't be parsed.
-- The README documents this at 3 rows (0.003%); the test we'll add
-- against stg_orders verifies the count hasn't drifted.
WHERE TRY_CAST(PURCHASE_TS_cleaned AS TIMESTAMP) IS NOT NULL

source.openbuild_dbt.bronze.orders_raw.name: orders_raw
source.openbuild_dbt.bronze.orders_raw.source_description: Raw data loaded directly from the source Excel file by scripts/load_bronze.py. Bronze tables preserve source values faithfully as VARCHAR; type casting and cleaning happen in the silver (staging) layer.
source.openbuild_dbt.bronze.orders_raw.description: One row per order from the source system. 108,127 rows covering Jan 2019 - Dec 2022. Contains both raw and pre-cleaned variants of several columns (e.g. PURCHASE_TS vs PURCHASE_TS_cleaned) preserved for audit lineage.

USER_ID.name: USER_ID
USER_ID.description: Identifier for the user who placed the order.

COUNTRY_CODE_cleaned.name: COUNTRY_CODE_cleaned
COUNTRY_CODE_cleaned.description: ISO country code after source-side cleaning.

PURCHASE_PLATFORM.name: PURCHASE_PLATFORM
PURCHASE_PLATFORM.description: Where the order was placed (website or mobile app).

source.openbuild_dbt.bronze.country_look.name: country_lookup_raw
source.openbuild_dbt.bronze.country_look.source_description: Raw data loaded directly from the source Excel file by scripts/load_bronze.py. Bronze tables preserve source values faithfully as VARCHAR; type casting and cleaning happen in the silver (staging) layer.
source.openbuild_dbt.bronze.country_look.description:
Country code to region mapping. 193 rows, 191 distinct country codes. Two known source defects, both surfaced by warn-level tests below and resolved in stg_country_lookup:
  1. One row has NULL COUNTRY_CODE with REGION='EMEA'
     (orphan row, excluded in silver)
  2. COUNTRY_CODE 'US' appears twice with conflicting regions
     'x' and 'North America' (deterministic dedup in silver,
     canonical region 'AMER')

COUNTRY_CODE.name: COUNTRY_CODE
COUNTRY_CODE.description: ISO country code. Source has 1 NULL row and 1 duplicate.

REGION.name: REGION
REGION.description: Regional rollup. May be NULL or junk ('x') in raw form. 26 source countries have NULL/junk regions; mapped to 'Unclassified' in silver.

macro.dbt_duckdb.duckdb__list_relations_.macro_sql:
{% macro duckdb__list_relations_without_caching(schema_relation) %}
  {% call statement('list_relations_without_caching', fetch_result=True) -%}
    select
      '{{ schema_relation.database }}' as database,
      table_name as name,
      table_schema as schema,
      CASE table_type
        WHEN 'BASE TABLE' THEN 'table'
        WHEN 'VIEW' THEN 'view'
        WHEN 'LOCAL TEMPORARY' THEN 'table'
        END as type
    from system.information_schema.tables
    where lower(table_schema) = '{{ schema_relation.schema | lower }}'
    and lower(table_catalog) = '{{ schema_relation.database | lower }}'
  {% endcall %}
  {{ return(load_result('list_relations_without_caching').table) }}
{% endmacro %}

macro.dbt_duckdb.duckdb__get_catalog.name: duckdb__get_catalog
macro.dbt_duckdb.duckdb__get_catalog.macro_sql:
{% macro duckdb__get_catalog(information_schema, schemas) -%}
  {%- call statement('catalog', fetch_result=True) -%}
    with relations AS (
      select
        t.table_name
        , t.database_name
        , t.schema_name
        , 'BASE TABLE' as table_type
        , {{ adapter.catalog_comment('t') }} as table_comment
      from duckdb_tables() t
      WHERE t.database_name = '{{ database }}'
      UNION ALL
      SELECT v.view_name as table_name
      , v.database_name
      , v.schema_name
      , 'VIEW' as table_type
      , {{ adapter.catalog_comment('v') }} as table_comment
      from duckdb_views() v
      WHERE v.database_name = '{{ database }}'
    )
    select
        '{{ database }}' as table_database,
        r.schema_name as table_schema,
        r.table_name,
        r.table_type,
        r.table_comment,
        c.column_name,
        c.column_index as column_index,
        c.data_type as column_type,
        {{ adapter.catalog_comment('c') }} as column_comment,
        '' as table_owner
    FROM relations r JOIN duckdb_columns() c ON r.schema_name = c.schema_name AND r.table_name = c.table_name
    WHERE (
        {%- for schema in schemas -%}
          upper(r.schema_name) = upper('{{ schema }}'){%- if not loop.last %} or {% endif -%}
        {%- endfor -%}
    )
    ORDER BY
        r.schema_name,
        r.table_name,
        c.column_index
  {%- endcall -%}
  {{ return(load_result('catalog').table) }}
{%- endmacro %}

doc.dbt.__overview__.block_contents:
### Welcome!

Welcome to the auto-generated documentation for your dbt project!

### Navigation

You can use the `Project` and `Database` navigation tabs on the left side of the window to explore the models
in your project.

#### Project Tab
The `Project` tab mirrors the directory structure of your dbt project. In this tab, you can see all of the
models defined in your dbt project, as well as models imported from dbt packages.

#### Database Tab
The `Database` tab also exposes your models, but in a format that looks more like a database explorer. This view
shows relations (tables and views) grouped into database schemas. Note that ephemeral models are _not_ shown
in this interface, as they do not exist in the database.

### Graph Exploration
You can click the blue icon on the bottom-right corner of the page to view the lineage graph of your models.

On model pages, you'll see the immediate parents and children of the model you're exploring. By clicking the `Expand`
button at the top-right of this lineage pane, you'll be able to see all of the models that are used to build,
or are built from, the model you're exploring.

Once expanded, you'll be able to use the `--select` and `--exclude` model selection syntax to filter the
models in the graph. For more information on model selection, check out the [dbt docs](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/model-selection-syntax).

Note that you can also right-click on models to interactively filter and explore the graph.

---

### More information

- Read the [dbt viewpoint](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/viewpoint)
- [Installation](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/installation)
- Join the [dbt Community](https://www.getdbt.com/community/) for questions and discussion
